content.text: Big-bang rewrites of AI systems routinely fail. Model dependencies, prompt chains, and evaluation datasets are tightly coupled in ways that make wholesale replacement dangerous. Incremental architecture evolution — replacing one slice of the system at a time while the rest keeps running — is the safer path. The strangler fig pattern, feature flags, and dual-write state migration are the three core techniques that make this possible.
What Incremental Architecture Evolution Is

Incremental architecture evolution is the practice of migrating an AI system to a new architecture by routing progressively larger slices of production traffic to the new version, measuring outcomes, and either advancing the rollout or rolling back — without ever taking the system down or deploying a replacement all at once.

The name comes from the strangler fig tree, which grows around a host tree and gradually replaces it. In software, the new architecture grows alongside the old one, handling increasing traffic until the old architecture can be safely removed.
Why Big-Bang Rewrites Fail for AI Systems
Standard software rewrites are risky. AI system rewrites are riskier because of three compounding factors:
Model dependencies:

prompts are written for a specific model version. Switching model providers or versions invalidates all prompt tuning done for the old model. A new architecture often implies a new model, which invalidates the evaluation baselines you built trust on.
Prompt dependencies:
system prompts, few-shot examples, and output parsers are brittle across model version boundaries. A change that passes offline eval may regress in edge cases only found in production traffic.
Evaluation gaps:

LLM quality is hard to measure offline. An eval set of 100 golden examples cannot capture the long tail of production queries. Incremental rollout exposes the new architecture to real traffic diversity before full commitment.
Benefits
Risk containment:
a 5% canary limits blast radius. A regression affects only 5% of users and can be rolled back by flipping a flag.
Real-traffic validation:
production query distributions surface edge cases that offline eval sets miss. You discover prompt failures before they affect all users.
Continuous delivery:
teams can merge and deploy architectural changes continuously rather than waiting for a big-bang cutover event.
Reversibility:
a feature flag is a one-second rollback. No redeployment required during an incident.
Cost tracking:
running both architectures in parallel with real traffic lets you measure the cost delta precisely before committing.
Trade-offs
Dual running cost:
operating two architectures in parallel doubles infrastructure spend during the migration period.
Dual-write complexity:
when state (memory stores, vector indices, conversation history) must be kept in sync across old and new architectures, the write path becomes complex and failure modes multiply.
Extended migration timelines:
incremental rollouts take weeks or months, not days. Teams must maintain both architectures during this window.
Observability overhead:
comparing old vs new architecture metrics requires disciplined tagging of all telemetry with architecture version labels.
Mental Model

Think of the migration as a controlled experiment with a kill switch. The proxy (feature flag or load balancer) is the experiment controller. The new architecture is the treatment group. The old architecture is the control group. The rollout percentage is the treatment allocation. Metrics are the outcome measures. The feature flag is the abort button.

You advance the experiment (increase traffic allocation) only when outcome metrics for the treatment group stay within acceptable bounds of the control group. You abort (flip flag to 0%) the instant any metric degrades beyond threshold.
How It Works: The Strangler Fig Pattern for AI
The strangler fig pattern applied to AI systems proceeds in three phases:
Proxy insertion:
add a routing layer in front of the existing architecture. All traffic flows through it unchanged. The new architecture exists but receives zero traffic (shadow mode only).
Gradual traffic shift:

use a feature flag to send 1%, then 5%, 10%, 25%, 50%, 100% of traffic to the new architecture. At each step, measure latency, error rate, cost, and quality metrics. Advance only when all metrics are within acceptable bounds.
Decommission:
once 100% of traffic runs on the new architecture and all rollback windows have passed, shut down the old architecture and remove the dual-write synchronization.
Architecture Diagram

content.text: Feature Flags for Architecture Switchover

Feature flag systems (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or a home-built flag store) are the control plane for traffic routing. A flag encodes the routing rule: which user segments, request types, or percentage of traffic should go to the new architecture.

# Feature flag-driven routing with LaunchDarkly
import ldclient
from ldclient.config import Config

ldclient.set_config(Config("sdk-key"))
ld = ldclient.get()

def route_request(request, user_id: str) -> str:
    """Route a request to old or new AI architecture based on feature flag."""
    context = ldclient.Context.builder(user_id).build()

    # Flag returns a string: 'v1' or 'v2'
    # Rollout is configured in the LaunchDarkly dashboard (e.g. 10% -> v2)
    arch_version = ld.variation("ai-arch-version", context, default="v1")

    if arch_version == "v2":
        return new_architecture_handler(request)
    else:
        return old_architecture_handler(request)

# Rollback: flip flag to 100% v1 in LaunchDarkly UI
# No redeployment needed — takes effect in < 200ms
With Unleash (open-source alternative), configure gradual rollouts using the
gradualRolloutUserId
strategy, which hashes user ID to produce a consistent, deterministic routing decision — the same user always sees the same architecture version.
Dual-Write Pattern for State Migration

When the old and new architectures share stateful resources — a vector store, a conversation memory database, or a user preference store — writes must go to both stores during the migration window. This keeps the old architecture able to serve rollback traffic even after the new architecture has been processing writes.

import asyncio
from typing import Any

class DualWriteMemoryStore:
    """
    During migration: writes go to both old (Pinecone) and new (Weaviate) stores.
    Reads come from whichever store the current architecture version uses.
    """
    def __init__(self, old_store, new_store, dual_write_enabled: bool = True):
        self.old_store = old_store
        self.new_store = new_store
        self.dual_write_enabled = dual_write_enabled

    async def write(self, key: str, value: Any) -> None:
        if self.dual_write_enabled:
            # Write to both stores concurrently
            # Failure in new store is logged but does not block the old store write
            old_task = asyncio.create_task(self.old_store.write(key, value))
            new_task = asyncio.create_task(self._safe_new_write(key, value))
            await old_task  # old store is the source of truth during migration
            await new_task  # new store failure is non-fatal
        else:
            # Post-migration: write to new store only
            await self.new_store.write(key, value)

    async def _safe_new_write(self, key: str, value: Any) -> None:
        try:
            await self.new_store.write(key, value)
        except Exception as e:
            # Log and alert but do not propagate
            logger.error("dual_write_new_store_failure", key=key, error=str(e))
            metrics.increment("dual_write.new_store.failures")

    async def read(self, key: str, arch_version: str = "v1") -> Any:
        if arch_version == "v2":
            return await self.new_store.read(key)
        return await self.old_store.read(key)
Canary Deployments and Rollback Triggers

A canary deployment sends a small percentage of traffic (typically 1-5%) to the new architecture while the rest continues to the stable version. The key is defining rollback triggers — metric thresholds that automatically (or manually) flip the routing back to the old architecture.

# Automated rollback trigger (run as a cron job or alerting rule)
import httpx
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RollbackTrigger:
    metric: str
    threshold_pct_above_baseline: float  # e.g. 0.05 = 5% above baseline
    window_minutes: int

ROLLBACK_TRIGGERS = [
    RollbackTrigger("error_rate", threshold_pct_above_baseline=0.05, window_minutes=5),
    RollbackTrigger("latency_p95_ms", threshold_pct_above_baseline=0.50, window_minutes=10),
    RollbackTrigger("cost_per_request_usd", threshold_pct_above_baseline=0.30, window_minutes=15),
]

async def check_rollback_needed(prometheus_url: str, canary_label: str) -> bool:
    """Return True if any rollback trigger is breached."""
    for trigger in ROLLBACK_TRIGGERS:
        baseline = await query_prometheus(
            prometheus_url,
            f'avg_over_time({trigger.metric}{{arch="v1"}}[{trigger.window_minutes}m])'
        )
        canary_val = await query_prometheus(
            prometheus_url,
            f'avg_over_time({trigger.metric}{{arch="v2"}}[{trigger.window_minutes}m])'
        )
        if canary_val > baseline * (1 + trigger.threshold_pct_above_baseline):
            logger.warning(
                "rollback_trigger_breached",
                metric=trigger.metric,
                baseline=baseline,
                canary=canary_val,
            )
            return True
    return False

async def execute_rollback(flag_client, flag_key: str) -> None:
    """Flip feature flag to 0% canary traffic immediately."""
    await flag_client.update_rollout_percentage(flag_key, percentage=0)
    await notify_oncall("Canary rollback executed — all traffic reverted to v1")
Measuring Success of Incremental Rollout
Track four metric categories in parallel for both old and new architecture during rollout:
Latency:
p50, p95, p99 response times tagged by
arch_version
. New architecture must not exceed 110% of old p95.
Error rate:
HTTP 5xx rate, model timeouts, parse failures. Threshold: no more than 1 percentage point above old architecture.
Cost delta:
token usage × price per token per request. For migrations to more capable (and pricier) models, set an explicit budget ceiling before starting rollout.
Quality:
thumbs up/down ratings, CSAT signals, or LLM judge scores on sampled production responses. Quality regressions are the hardest to detect automatically — sample aggressively.
When to Use Incremental Architecture Evolution
Migrating from one LLM provider to another (GPT-4 to Claude, for example) where prompt behavior differences are uncertain.
Replacing a monolithic agent loop with a multi-agent architecture, where the output contract must stay identical during transition.
Switching from a keyword search retrieval system to dense vector retrieval, where recall quality changes are hard to predict offline.
Upgrading the memory store schema (from flat JSON to a structured episodic store) without losing existing user memory data.
Real-World Example

A customer support AI system runs on GPT-4 Turbo with a flat vector store. The team wants to migrate to Claude 3.5 Sonnet with a new chunking strategy and Weaviate as the vector backend. The migration plan:
Week 1: insert a routing proxy. Shadow-mode all requests through the new architecture (log outputs, never serve them to users).
Week 2: enable dual-write to Weaviate. All new conversation memory goes to both stores.
Week 3: canary at 5% of production traffic. Monitor for 48 hours. Quality sampling from 50 responses in each group.
Week 4-5: ramp to 25%, 50%, 100% in 48-hour increments if metrics are green.
Week 6: decommission old vector store and GPT-4 integration. Remove dual-write path.
Common Mistakes
Skipping shadow mode:
going straight from 0% to 5% canary without first validating that the new architecture even runs correctly on real production traffic shapes is a common shortcut that leads to immediate rollbacks.
No quality signal during rollout:

tracking only latency and error rate misses quality regressions. A model that is fast and reliable but gives worse answers is still a regression. Sample and score production responses throughout the rollout.
One-way dual write:

writing only from the new architecture to the new store, and not backfilling historical data from the old store. When rolling back, the old architecture is missing state written during the migration window.
Removing old architecture too early:
decommissioning the old architecture the same day you reach 100% traffic is risky. Keep it running in standby for at least one week to ensure a clean rollback path remains available.
Best Practices
Define rollback triggers before starting the rollout. Agree on thresholds for each metric and automate the check. Never rely on manual observation of dashboards as the primary signal.
Tag all telemetry with
arch_version
. Without version labels on every metric, comparing old and new architectures in production is nearly impossible.
Keep rollout increments small. Go 1% → 5% → 25% → 100%, not 1% → 100%. Each increment is a validation step.
Run canary phases for at least 24 hours each to cover daily usage patterns — peak and off-peak traffic have different query distributions.
Document the dual-write contract: which fields are synced, which are not, and what the source of truth is for each field during the migration window.
Performance and Scalability

The routing proxy adds one network hop to every request. Use an in-process flag evaluation library (not an HTTP-based flag service) to keep this overhead under 1ms. LaunchDarkly's SDK caches flag state locally and evaluates in microseconds.
Dual-write to two stores doubles write latency if done sequentially. Use concurrent writes (Python
asyncio.gather
or Go goroutines) and make the new store write non-blocking so it does not add to the critical path.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Feature flags:
LaunchDarkly, Unleash (open-source), Flagsmith (open-source), AWS AppConfig, GrowthBook.
Canary traffic management:
Argo Rollouts (Kubernetes-native), Flagger, AWS CodeDeploy canary mode, Nginx split_clients module.
Metrics comparison:
Prometheus with recording rules by
arch_version
label; Grafana dashboard with version-comparison panels.
Quality sampling:
LangSmith, Braintrust, or Honeycomb for sampled response logging and human review queues.
Comparison with Alternatives

Production AI systems with SLA obligations

Medium

Stateless services with identical data stores

Very low

Offline validation only; cannot validate latency under load
Security and Reliability

Ensure the routing proxy does not leak prompt content between architecture versions. If the old and new architectures use different prompt encryption schemes, the proxy must not log or forward raw prompt text.
The dual-write path increases the attack surface: a vulnerability in the new store write path must not propagate to the old store. Isolate write credentials.
Feature flag evaluation must be deterministic for security-sensitive routing. Use user-ID-based hashing, not random sampling, to avoid a user seeing different architectures within the same session.

During canary phases, ensure PII and sensitive data flow through the same data residency controls in both architectures. A migration that accidentally routes EU user data to a US-hosted model instance violates GDPR.
Key Takeaways
Big-bang rewrites fail for AI systems because model dependencies, prompt tuning, and eval baselines are all invalidated at once. Incremental evolution contains this risk.
The strangler fig pattern routes traffic gradually to the new architecture, starting with shadow mode and advancing in measured steps.
Feature flags (LaunchDarkly, Unleash) are the kill switch — a one-second rollback with no redeployment.
Dual-write keeps both architecture versions in sync on state, so rollback remains possible even after the new architecture has processed writes.
Define rollback triggers before starting rollout: error rate, latency p95, cost delta, and quality score thresholds.
Tag all telemetry with arch_version. Without version labels, you cannot compare old vs new in production.
Keep the old architecture running in standby for at least one week after reaching 100% traffic — before decommissioning.
